replies.title: Re: Eleusinian Mysteries
replies.content:
Deborah Bender here.

I attended the ATC Spring Mysteries about twenty years ago. It was already a well established event then, dating back to perhaps the 1970s.

I had a basis for comparison, because my primary witchcraft tradition, NROOGD, had been doing a version of those Mysteries as an annual participatory ritual/ritual theater piece in the fall, since about 1970, in various coastal locations. The NROOGD version was a three hour all in one combination of the Lesser and Greater Mysteries.

ATC enacted the Lesser and Greater Mysteries as separate rites, which is truer to the historical mysteries. As a priestess of the NROOGD Eleusinian, I was given the status of epoptai(sp?) and admitted to the Greater Mysteries without having previously attend ATC's Lesser Mysteries.

The ATC version took place on an island with an ideal set of indoor and outdoor locations, including a two story building with a classical facade which served very well as a set for the Underworld. The rite took place episodically over three days, which gave time to include versions of some of the preparatory parts of the EM which were public enough to be known to us in some detail, such as a (symbolic) piglet sacrifice.

There were shrines set up to various Greek deities associated with this mystery, and time for people to visit the shrines on their own. The connection of the Emolpid (spelling?) family as priesthood of the rite was acted out as a playlet.

As a mystes, I swore not to reveal what took place in the Telesterion.

The people who designed this ritual had to deal with some problems that NROOGD and any other modern reconstructionist group has to reckon with. The things that were seen and done which so impressed the Hellenes and Romans drew partly on cultural references which are dimmer or unknown to most of us. We come to this mystery with somewhat different ideas and concerns about the afterlife and different expectations about our relationships with deities than the ancients. They were on the whole in better physical condition to engage in long processional walks and all night dancing. We don't know what the greatest revelation was.

As someone who has acted both priestess roles and representations of Persephone and Demeter in the pared down version of the Eleusinian Mysteries which NROOGD used to do, I think the ATC had superior production values, NROOGD had better music, and both were challenged by where the boundaries between ritual and ritual theater lie.

A lot of work goes into this, and it won't survive forever, so if you can, go to Spring Mysteries.

replies.title: Re: Eleusinian Mysteries
replies.content: Thank you! It definitely sounds like it's very close to what I am looking for.

comments.content: Can you explain a little more about an astral body birth bringing a person fully into society and a mental body birth often taking someone out of it?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The astral body is the body in which emotions (as distinct from passions), imagination, dreams, and concrete thoughts (i.e., thoughts in words or images) function. That body finishes being born around the age of puberty, and once the person learns how to use it -- a tangled process these days, which is why adolescence is such a rough road -- they are fully functioning members of society, since society is largely mediated by astral phenomena of the kind just listed.

Once the mental sheath turns into a mental body, by contrast, the individual can function in the realm of meaning, purpose, and value, not just the reflections of these things in the astral. Since most people don't function on this level, the person who does is always at least a little odd, by the standards of their neighbors!

comments.title: Developing the mental body
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

1. You have taught that developing the mental sheath into the mental body is done primarily through abstract thinking, especially through such practices as discursive meditation. I'm wondering what role ethical improvement/moral purification plays in this process. Many religions emphasize that moral purification is essential to attaining the goal of the spiritual path. What does occultism teach about this?

2. Just a terminological question. When you talk about "enlightenment", does this refer to the condition of having developed the mental body?

3. I recall you saying elsewhere that you're planning to release a book dedicated to discursive meditation. How far along are you in this project? When might this book be released?

Thanks so much for all you do!

replies.title: Re: Developing the mental body
replies.content:
1) Ethics are much more complex than they look at first glance. You can follow some formal set of behavioral rules and still be a genuinely nasty person, for example, and you can also keep a strict morality at a very low level of consciousness. As the mental sheath develops, it becomes possible to approach ethics from another level, a level of meaning, value, and purpose; this is what guides the truly saintly -- but you can't teach this to someone who isn't ready to learn it. Thus moral improvement is a side effect of spiritual development, as I see it, and not a path to spiritual development; the great failing of the religious traditions of the Piscean age lies, as I see it, in losing track of this, and thinking that if you just bully people into not doing bad things, they will achieve spiritual awakening. Not so!

2) Yes, though I'm becoming increasingly uncomfortable with the word "enlightenment."

3) It's still in rough outline and I have no idea when it'll be finisyhed.

replies.title: Re: Developing the mental body
replies.content: 2) Is that because modern Western rationalists have totally disfigured its more traditional meaning(s)? Also, that different spiritual traditions define the word very differently?

replies.title: Re: Developing the mental body
replies.content: It's a crude translation of much more subtle words in other languages. The Japanese word satori, for example, comes from a verb meaning "to perceive, to understand, to comprehend." The Sanskrit word samadhi, by contrast, comes from words that literally mean "to connect" or "to bring together." The concept has been surrounded by too much woowoo and handwaving in the Western world.

replies.title: Re: Developing the mental body
replies.content:
And English is a pretty crippled language for a lot of translations. I'm finding that in my study of Koine Greek and Hebrew verses. For example, the word that gets translated as "fear" in English, comes from Hebrew "'yr "which also includes awe, respect, adoration, and yes, some fear. 'Yr is the attitude you have towards a good, just, and mighty king. "Fear" is the attitude you have toward a lion that wants to eat you.

- Cicada Grove

replies.title: Re: Developing the mental body
replies.content: Good heavens. You've just clarified a fascinating detail about the novel I Never Promised You A Rose Garden, which was popular when I was a teenager.

replies.title: Re: Developing the mental body
replies.content:
My study buddy sent me the link to this paper about 'yr' back in 2023.
(I misspelled it, it's " yr' "):

https://bibleinterp.arizona.edu/sites/bibleinterp.arizona.edu/files/images/Lasater4.pdf

Casually, the authors drop a different revelation on Page 3, paragraph 3 to page 4, paragraph 1.
The entire doctrine of "emotions" was created by a couple of Scottish philosophers in the 1700's.

- Cicada Grove

replies.title: Re: Developing the mental body
replies.content:
Makes a ton of sense!

Might be related to the reason why I prefer to leave the Greek word Nous untranslated.

"Intellect"? blargh....

replies.title: Re: Developing the mental body

replies.title: Re: Developing the mental body
replies.content: It's a source of great amusement to me that English actually has a word borrowed from Babylonian: abyss. (It's abzu in the cuneiform texts.) English is the least chaste of all the world's languages -- it's got all the reticent purity of a cat in heat.

replies.title: Re: Developing the mental body
replies.content: Can confirm, English may be the most mongrelized of the major world languages!

replies.title: Re: Developing the mental body

Letting people grow into salvation, so to speak, seems like a far better approach than the carrot & stick of heaven & hell.

comments.title: Releasing an Emotion
comments.content:
Good morning JMG,

Last night I did some journaling and then quiet, prayerful listening about the question 'What am I doing with my life?' I was thinking specifically of my creative pursuits and why, despite demonstrating discipline, willpower, and capability in other areas of life, I've had a pattern for several years now of putting off the creative work I dream of in favour of almost anything else- socializing, exercising, working extra hours at my job, doing the dishes. Last night I journaled just a short while and then went to bed early, with my ears open, so to speak.

I woke at 3 am and quickly became aware that I was full of rage. Hatred, actually. Hatred for the world, for the people burning up all the oil, for the people pushing medical tyranny, for the people sludging up the world with bad art and bad taste. I lay in bed seething, filled with the desire for revenge, and my revenge would consist in withholding my creative gifts and never giving them to the world. It was a remarkable and unpleasant experience, like turning over the compost pile and instead of rich, black earth, seeing an indecipherable shape swarming with maggots. I got up from my bed and had the brief urge to find something I had created or nurtured and destroy it, out of spite. (I did not do so).

I did not know I was carrying all that around inside, but it could explain a lot. I imagine you're going to advise me to journal further about this important clue, and I will, but is there anything else I can do to release or transform this hatred? I am already working on the Octagon exercises but haven't yet come across this topic.

In case it's relevant, I've been experimenting with total sexual abstinence for some time now, with the intention of clearing my mind and making myself available for other forms of energy. Today marks seven weeks of abstinence.

I think I know what my next step is (journaling more) but I'd be grateful for any light you can shed on this experience.

replies.title: Re: Releasing an Emotion
replies.content: Well done. That sort of thing is much more common than you may realize. The thing you need to remember at this point is that anger is a secondary emotion -- we feel anger and hatred because it's easier than the primary emotion it covers up, which is usually grief, shame, or fear (or some combination of these). You won't be able to release the rage until you get to the primary emotion underneath it, and experience, accept, and release that.

replies.title: Re: Releasing an Emotion
replies.content:
Gee, thanks!

Okay, grief, shame, fear are the primary emotions. Hatred is a secondary one? It felt pretty primal, so I figured I had got to the root of something. But if there's another layer hiding under it, I'll tunnel deeper and see what I get.

replies.title: Re: Releasing an Emotion
replies.content: Hatred and anger are secondary because they're defensive. Back in prehistoric times, if you were being hunted by a leopard, your fear gave way to rage and hatred, and those surged up and gave you the strength and fierceness you needed to skewer the beast with a stone-tipped spear.  We still have those reactions, even though sticking a spear through something isn't very useful in most situations these days, and in most people it spreads and can get triggered by grief or shame as well.

replies.title: Re: Releasing an Emotion
replies.content:
Okay, so my next step is to look for the primary emotion (or combination of primary emotions) that hatred and rage are covering up, then experience, accept, and release that hidden emotion. Sounds straightforward... though not necessarily easy.

Out of curiosity, when you said 'That sort of thing is much more common than you may realize,' did you mean that hiding primary emotions under secondary emotions generally is common, or that doing so in relation to personal creativity is common?

I've heard all kinds of tips about how to get around creative blocks, but not much about where those blocks come from. Understanding the origin seems a lot more useful to me. My block was at first so baffling and then this middle-of-the-night emotional clue was so surprising that my curiosity is now thoroughly piqued.

Thanks again for all your work on Magic Monday!

replies.title: Re: Releasing an Emotion
replies.content: Primary emotions being hidden under secondary emotions is all but universal, and quite often you get a third layer on top of that -- depression, for example, is far more often not a tertiary emotion over the top of anger, and the anger covers some primary emotion. (That's why depression is so difficult to resolve; you have to work all the way down, and most people won't.) That sort of thing as a barrier to personal creativity is nearly as common. If more of us did the work and released and resolved those old suppressed emotions, there'd be much more creativity than we see around us these days.

replies.title: Re: Breath Work and Martial Arts
replies.content:
I have been practicing bjj for 3+ years now. there are no particular breathing techniques - everyone can breathe the way it works for them. my humble advice to the person asking would be to always breathe through the nose during sparrings - this way you are not going to gas out fast and would be forced to wrestle more slowly, which is exactly what you need to spot your mistakes, your opponent's actions and your surroundings (trust me you will want to monitor your surroundings because no matter how spacy your gym will be, there is always a risk the neighboring couple of wrestlers fall on you or your opponent). this will be very hard to implement at the very beginning of your training because you will be scared and your instincts will make you be spazzy and move fast. this is totally normal. everyone goes through this. it is just a matter of practice like all other skills.

if you are interested in bjj, please give it a try and have fun! this is an amazing journey which teaches discipline, control, respect and how to be humble (cause there is always someone better than you and also when you think you know something, usually quite fast there is someone to show you that you really do not know anything). i personally have never regretted starting it.

another piece of advice would be not to ignore strength and conditioning trainings to do in parallel with bjj (at least once a week). this way you will prevent a lot of injuries!

the question i had in that regard - martial arts involve a lot of close contact between bodies. in bjj there is a position called side control, where one person is flat on his back, and another one lays somewhat perpendicular on top of them, pinning them to the ground. in this position basically the solar plexuses of two people are located one against the other. are the interactions of such nature have any effect on etheric or astral bodies?

comments.title: Shinto
comments.content:
After almost thirty years in Japan, I have finally gotten around to learning about Shinto thought, beyond the surface level of going through the motions. As is my usual practice, I started by picking up a book on the subject.

Interestingly, there are a lot of similarities to the western occult tradition. There is the concept of the planes, the divine spark and evolution of the soul, magical practices of banishing and invocation, and many others. Of course, there is no central or authoritative doctrine, like you are always saying of modern druidry.

I can think of various explanations for the similarities. Maybe because we are all dealing with the same underlying reality; or perhaps there are remnants of an older (dare I say original?) tradition common to both. More recent borrowing, one way or both, also seems a possibility.

I'd be interested to hear your thoughts (or anybody else's) about connections between Shinto and the kind of western occult thinking that you write about. I gather that Shinto has been a persistent interest of yours, as well.

replies.title: Re: Shinto
replies.content:
With Shinto you're dealing with a tradition that has many layers of esotericism. There's the original, primordial core, which goes all the way back to the Jomon period; there are borrowings from Chinese Taoist magic, brought over in the Yamato period and largely embodied in Onmyodo; you have the extraordinarily rich esoteric Buddhist traditions that arrived in the Heian period; put in a small island chain, cover, and bake for a thousand years, then take out of the oven at the beginning the Meiji period and top with a lively mix of Western spiritual and esoteric traditions, and there you are!

The fact of the matter is that all these borrowings took place because they worked, and they worked because they correspond to a shared reality. I once had a long and lively conversation about spiritual matters with the chief priest of a Shinto shrine in Washington State. It quickly became clear to both of us that despite the differences in terminology, we were talking about not only shared theories but identical experiences. Thus I consider Shinto teachings a very solid guide to the spiritual world.

replies.title: Re: Shinto
replies.content: The many layers of borrowing was a topic addressed in the book I am reading. I'm very pleased to discover that something like the teachings and traditions I have been looking into in recent years may be accessible very close to home (there aren't many GD lodges in Japan).
